extraction_notes: Operations distilled from Aider's repo-map command surface plus generalized primitives every coder-agent harness will need. Operation names are conventions in SKILL.md; the underlying behaviors are framework-agnostic.

candidates_considered.name: map.build
candidates_considered.rationale: Fundamental: every harness needs a 'create initial map' entry point. Maps to Aider implicit-on-start behavior.

candidates_considered.name: map.refresh
candidates_considered.rationale: Stale maps cause hallucinated symbols. Explicit refresh is the most common dilemma trigger.

candidates_considered.name: map.scope
candidates_considered.rationale: Monorepo scoping is the dominant context-overflow mitigation. Maps to Aider --subtree-only.

candidates_considered.name: map.print
candidates_considered.rationale: Auditability is repo-map's structural advantage over RAG. Worth an explicit op.

candidates_considered.name: map.locate
candidates_considered.rationale: The 'use' phase of the SOP. Backed by the 70.3% benchmark. This is *the* operation, not a minor utility.

candidates_considered.name: map.budget_set
candidates_considered.rationale: Token-budget tuning is a first-class concern given the 25k attention threshold.

candidates_considered.name: map.ignore_add
candidates_considered.rationale: Most common 'first response' to oversized maps. Cheap, high-leverage.

candidates_considered.name: map.diff
candidates_considered.rationale: Unique to repo-map (RAG can't do this). Surfaces silent drift between sessions.

candidates_considered.name: map.merge_multiple_repos
candidates_considered.rationale: Cross-repo is out of scope for v0.1. Noted as open question in R2.

candidates_considered.name: map.semantic_overlay
candidates_considered.rationale: Hybrid repo-map + embedding ranking is speculative; no benchmark support yet.

candidates_considered.name: map.symbol_search
candidates_considered.rationale: Better left to grep/IDE; not a repo-map specific primitive.

candidates_considered.name: map.cache_warm
candidates_considered.rationale: Implementation detail of build/refresh; not a user-facing operation.

operation_design_principles: Each op cites at least one Aider source URL or generalized engineering rationale.
Trigger/Action/Output/Evidence shape forces concrete usage examples.
Names are framework-agnostic but follow Aider's mental groupings for transferability.
Operations are composable: map.build → map.locate → (write-set decision) → map.budget_set(0) is a canonical pipeline.

dilemma_cases_extracted.label: wrong_file_edited
dilemma_cases_extracted.primary_evidence: Aider's write-set / read-set separation design; 70.3% on SWE-Bench Lite as baseline [aider.chat/2024/05/22/swe-bench-lite.html]

surprising_findings_log: Aider's 70.3% on SWE-Bench Lite was achieved with NO code execution at reasoning time, NO embeddings, and NO network calls. This single configuration may be the highest-ROI public datapoint in 2024 coder-agent design.
The repo-map blog post (2023-10-22) uses 'graph ranking algorithm' — not 'PageRank' — even though community writeups universally call it PageRank. Worth noting in R1.
The default --map-tokens is only 1k, much smaller than most agent implementations assume. The signal-density premium is high.

Aider's design separates 'navigation' (repo-map gives the LLM a view) from 'write authorization' (only /add-ed files are editable). Most newer agents collapse these, which correlates with the 'wrong file edited' failure mode.
open_questions: What is the floor of map quality beneath which the harness should refuse to operate?
Are there language-specific PageRank-edge weights that outperform uniform edges?
Does map staleness cost more than the cost of full rebuild for repos under N files? (worth a microbenchmark)
